meta.name: Parks & Recreation

hero.lead: A plain-language navigator to Maui County Code Title 13 (Parks & Recreation) — Chapter 13.04A Parks & Recreational Facilities: how to get a park permit, the rules for camping and pavilion/facility use, and the general park prohibitions. Administered by the Department of Parks & Recreation. Sections marked expanding are being filled in; nothing here is legal advice.

sections.title: ★ Parcel lookup
sections.note: Optional context — enter a Maui County TMK to see designations from the Hawaiʻi Statewide GIS. For an actual park permit or facility reservation, use the County's Park Permits page (linked in the result).

source.html: Live designations: Hawaiʻi Statewide GIS. Park permits: County Park Permits. No data is stored or fabricated.

sections.title: 1 · Get a park permit
sections.note: Any activity, event, class, or program in a County park or facility needs a permit from the department first (Ch. 13.04A, Art. III).

steps.name: Pick your permit type
steps.what: Choose the right permit for your activity (camping, event, class, commercial ocean recreation, general use — see the table below).
steps.pay: Expense: none to choose · paid by: you (time)

steps.name: Apply to Parks & Recreation
steps.what: Submit the written application to the Department of Parks & Recreation before the activity; camping applicants must be 18 or older.
steps.pay: Expense: permit fee · paid by: applicant

steps.name: Carry your permit
steps.what: Keep a copy of the approved permit with you during the activity (required while camping).
steps.pay: Expense: comply with conditions · paid by: permit holder

position.label: ⚖️ Position — James Langford (analysis, not law; a stance beside the rule)
position.text: Parks are the people's shared ʻāina. Permits should protect access and the land, not ration it — keep resident, cultural, and family use easy and low-cost, and reserve fees and limits for commercial or high-impact uses.

source.html: Maui County Code Ch. 13.04A, Art. III (Permits) + County Park Permits.

rows.v: Overnight camping at a designated park

source.html: Maui County Code Ch. 13.04A (Parks & Recreational Facilities): camping §13.04A.200/.210, permits Art. III, prohibitions §13.04A.070.

sections.title: 3 · Who pays — the charter lens
sections.note: Labeled analysis — parks read through two charters. Not adopted law; an interpretive lens beside the Code.
sections.mauiCharter: Under the Maui County Charter, the Department of Parks & Recreation maintains parks and facilities and permits their use; fees offset maintenance and limit conflicts between uses. The Charter frames parks as public assets the department stewards.
sections.sovereignCharter: Under the Sovereign Charter (12 Stones) lens, the parks, shoreline, and gathering places are communal ʻāina held for all generations; access for residents, cultural practice, and ʻohana is a birthright. The lens favors free or low-cost resident access and pricing the burden onto commercial users who profit from the commons.

position.label: ⚖️ Position — analysis, not law
position.text: Keep the parks open and cheap for the people; charge the commercial operators who monetize them. Permits should guard the ʻāina and resolve conflicts, not gate residents out of their own commons. Same title, read two ways.

sections.text: Free now (no account): this page, the parcel lookup, the permit navigator, and the permit-type table are all Tier 1 — free. Coming — Tier 3 (verified tools): a permit-readiness checklist and an AI parks assistant will be verification-gated via the free govOS beta portal (identity verified with Stripe Identity — free, no charge); only verified-status is stored, never the raw ID. No secret keys (environment references only); any permit payment happens on the County's own system.
